name: Cottus
shortDescription: One of the Hecatoncheires, giants with 100 hands and 50 heads, imprisoned by Uranus and later freed by Zeus to aid in the Titanomachy.
description: Cottus is one of the three Hecatoncheires (meaning 'hundred-handed ones') in Greek mythology, monstrous giants born to Gaia (Earth) and Uranus (Sky). He, along with his brothers Briareus and Gyes, possessed immense strength, each having one hundred hands and fifty heads. Uranus, fearing their power, imprisoned them in Tartarus. Their imprisonment caused Gaia great pain, leading her to plot against Uranus with her Titan offspring. During the Titanomachy, the great war between the Titans and the Olympian gods, Zeus, advised by Gaia, freed the Hecatoncheires from Tartarus. Their unparalleled strength and ability to hurl hundreds of rocks at once proved crucial in the Olympians' victory. After the war, Cottus and his brothers were tasked with guarding the defeated Titans within Tartarus, ensuring they remained imprisoned.
subtitle: The Hundred-Handed Guardian of Tartarus
appearance: Cottus is described as a massive giant, possessing one hundred hands and fifty heads. His physical appearance inspires fear and awe, reflecting his immense power and monstrous nature. He is a formidable figure, capable of overwhelming any opponent.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Cottus's origin is directly linked to the primordial deities Gaia and Uranus. Born as one of the Hecatoncheires, his birth was a source of both immense power and fear for Uranus. The myth of his imprisonment and subsequent liberation highlights the themes of power, rebellion, and the cyclical nature of cosmic order. His role in the Titanomachy is pivotal; his strength turned the tide of the war in favor of the Olympians. Hesiod's Theogony provides the primary account of Cottus's origins and involvement in these events, establishing his place within the Greek pantheon.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Cottus's role is primarily defined by his participation in the Titanomachy and his subsequent assignment as a guard in Tartarus. His significance lies in his representation of raw, untamed power, a force that could sway the balance of cosmic conflicts. He embodies the idea that even monstrous beings can be instrumental in upholding order, albeit under the command of the Olympian gods. As a guardian of Tartarus, he symbolizes the containment of chaos and the perpetual threat of the Titans' resurgence.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Cottus's hundred hands and fifty heads can be interpreted as symbols of overwhelming power and comprehensive awareness. His imprisonment represents the suppression of primal forces, while his liberation signifies their potential to be harnessed for a greater purpose. The Hecatoncheires, as a group, can be seen as representing the raw, untamed aspects of nature, capable of both destruction and creation. His loyalty to Zeus after being freed suggests the possibility of even the most monstrous beings aligning with order and justice.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Cottus, while not as widely known as other figures in Greek mythology, remains a significant character within the broader narrative of the Titanomachy and the establishment of the Olympian order. His depiction as a monstrous giant with immense strength has influenced portrayals of similar figures in literature and art. In modern popular culture, the concept of multi-limbed or multi-headed beings often draws inspiration from figures like Cottus, representing overwhelming power or chaotic potential. His story serves as a reminder of the ancient Greeks' fascination with primal forces and their role in shaping the cosmos.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Primary source detailing the birth of Cottus and his role in the Titanomachy.

sources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive account of Greek myths, including the story of the Hecatoncheires.

associations.name: Tartarus
associations.significance: Cottus is assigned to guard the Titans imprisoned in Tartarus.

associations.name: Rocks
associations.significance: He hurls rocks with his many hands during the Titanomachy.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Briareos
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Another of the Hecatoncheires, similar in form and function to Cottus.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Hayagriva
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A deity often depicted with multiple heads or limbs, representing immense power and knowledge.

companions.name: Briareus
companions.description: Another of the Hecatoncheires, sharing the same parentage and physical characteristics as Cottus.

companions.name: Gyes
companions.description: Another of the Hecatoncheires, sharing the same parentage and physical characteristics as Cottus.

companions.name: Zeus
companions.description: The king of the Olympian gods, who freed Cottus and his brothers to aid in the Titanomachy.

cultural.modernLegacy: Cottus's image as a powerful, multi-limbed giant continues to inspire fantasy literature and art, representing raw strength and untamed power.
